questions.question: The unit interval (0,1) is divided at a point U chosen uniformly distributed over (0,1) into two disjoint subintervals. The expected length of the subinterval that contains 0.6 is __________ (rounded off to two decimal places).

understand.plain: A random point U splits (0,1) into (0,U) and (U,1). We want the expected length of whichever piece contains the fixed point 0.6. If U falls below 0.6 the right piece (U,1) contains 0.6; if U is above 0.6 the left piece (0,U) contains 0.6. We integrate each case over the uniform density.

keywords.explain: A random variable U ~ Uniform(0,1) has constant density f(u) = 1 for u in (0,1) and 0 elsewhere.
keywords.example: Tossing a dart uniformly at a unit rod; every position is equally likely.

keywords.explain: E[g(U)] = integral of g(u) * f(u) du over the support. Here f(u) = 1 so it simplifies to integral of g(u) du.
The length of the subinterval containing 0.6 depends on whether U < 0.6 or U > 0.6, giving a piecewise function of U.
keywords.example: If U = 0.3, the right subinterval (0.3, 1) contains 0.6 with length 0.7.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 520 130' width='520' height='130'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='40' y='55' width='440' height='20' rx='4' class='bg'/><rect x='40' y='55' width='220' height='20' rx='4' class='hi'/><line x1='40' y1='45' x2='40' y2='85' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='480' y1='45' x2='480' y2='85' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='260' y1='40' x2='260' y2='90' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><line x1='304' y1='45' x2='304' y2='85' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='304' cy='65' r='5' fill='#D85A30'/><text x='40' y='105' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>0</text><text x='260' y='105' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>U</text><text x='304' y='105' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle' fill='#D85A30'>0.6</text><text x='480' y='105' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>1</text><text x='150' y='48' class='tag mut' text-anchor='middle'>left piece (0, U)</text><text x='370' y='48' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>right piece (U, 1)</text><text x='304' y='25' class='tag' text-anchor='middle' fill='#D85A30'>fixed point</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A unit line segment split at random point U; the fixed point 0.6 lies in the right subinterval when U is less than 0.6.

given.aim: Find E[length of subinterval containing 0.6] when U ~ Uniform(0,1) splits (0,1).

terms.connects: Determines which piece is longer and which contains 0.6

terms.meaning: Length of the subinterval that contains 0.6 as a function of U
terms.example: If U = 0.3 < 0.6, piece (0.3,1) contains 0.6, so L = 0.7
terms.connects: Piecewise: L = (1-U) when U < 0.6, L = U when U > 0.6

terms.meaning: Expected length, computed by integrating L(u) * f(u) = L(u) * 1 over [0,1]
terms.example: Split into two integrals at u = 0.6

given.plan: Write L(u) as a piecewise function, split the integral at u = 0.6, evaluate each part, and add.

steps.title: Define the piecewise length function
steps.apply: When U = u < 0.6, the subinterval (u, 1) contains 0.6 with length 1 - u. When U = u > 0.6, the subinterval (0, u) contains 0.6 with length u.
steps.note: The event U = 0.6 has probability 0 and can be ignored.

steps.title: Set up the expectation integral
steps.formula_raw: E[L] = integral_0^1 L(u) * 1 du = integral_0^0.6 (1-u) du + integral_0.6^1 u du
steps.apply: Since U ~ Uniform(0,1) has density f(u) = 1, E[L] = integral_0^0.6 (1-u) du + integral_0.6^1 u du.
steps.note: Split at 0.6 because the formula for L changes there.

steps.title: Add both parts
steps.note: This is within the accepted range 0.70 to 0.80.

questions.formula_note: Only the basic probability integral formula is needed; L is expressed as a piecewise function and integrated over the uniform density.

questions.question: Let X be a random variable uniformly distributed in the interval [0, 2] and Y be a random variable uniformly distributed in the interval [0, 2]. If X and Y are independent of each other, the probability P(X + Y <= 1) is __________ (rounded off to three decimal places).

understand.plain: X and Y are independent, each Uniform(0,2). Their joint distribution is uniform over the square [0,2] x [0,2] with total area 4. We need the probability that X + Y is at most 1, which is the area of the triangle {(x,y) : x >= 0, y >= 0, x+y <= 1} divided by the total area 4.

keywords.explain: Two independent Uniform(0,2) variables have joint density f(x,y) = (1/2)(1/2) = 1/4 over the square [0,2] x [0,2].
keywords.example: Any unit-area region in the square has probability 1/4.

keywords.explain: For a uniform joint distribution, P(event) = (area of event region) / (total area of support).
The region x + y <= 1 with x, y >= 0 is a right triangle with legs of length 1, so area = (1/2)(1)(1) = 0.5.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 300 300' width='300' height='300'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='40' y='20' width='200' height='200' class='bg'/><polygon points='40,220 90,220 40,170' class='hi'/><line x1='40' y1='20' x2='40' y2='230' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='30' y1='220' x2='250' y2='220' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='40' y='245' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>0</text><text x='90' y='245' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>1</text><text x='140' y='245' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>1.5</text><text x='240' y='245' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>2</text><text x='25' y='220' class='sub ink' text-anchor='end'>0</text><text x='25' y='170' class='sub ink' text-anchor='end'>1</text><text x='25' y='120' class='sub mut' text-anchor='end'>1.5</text><text x='25' y='20' class='sub ink' text-anchor='end'>2</text><text x='55' y='205' class='tag' fill='#7F77DD' text-anchor='middle'>A=0.5</text><text x='155' y='120' class='lbl mut' text-anchor='middle'>total</text><text x='155' y='138' class='lbl mut' text-anchor='middle'>area=4</text><text x='155' y='270' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>X axis</text><text x='10' y='120' class='sub ink' transform='rotate(-90,10,120)' text-anchor='middle'>Y axis</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The 2x2 square is the joint support; the shaded triangle in the corner has area 0.5, giving P = 0.5/4 = 0.125.

given.aim: Find P(X + Y <= 1) where X and Y are independent Uniform(0,2).

terms.meaning: The square [0,2] x [0,2] with total area 4
terms.example: Every sub-region has probability equal to its area divided by 4

terms.meaning: The triangle {(x,y): x >= 0, y >= 0, x + y <= 1}
terms.connects: Its area divided by 4 gives the required probability

terms.meaning: P(event) = area(event region) / area(total support) for uniform distributions

given.plan: Identify the joint support square, find the area of the triangular region where X + Y <= 1, then divide by the total area 4.

steps.title: Identify the joint density
steps.apply: Since X and Y are independent, each Uniform(0,2) with density 1/2, the joint density is f(x,y) = 1/4 over the square [0,2] x [0,2].
steps.note: Total probability over the square is (1/4)*4 = 1, as required.

steps.title: Identify the favorable region
steps.apply: Since x, y >= 0 and x + y <= 1, the region is a right triangle with vertices at (0,0), (1,0), and (0,1). Note: the constraint x + y <= 1 with x,y >= 0 already forces x <= 1 and y <= 1, well within [0,2].
steps.note: The line x + y = 1 intersects the axes at (1,0) and (0,1).

steps.title: Compute the area of the triangle
steps.apply: The right triangle has base = 1 along the x-axis and height = 1 along the y-axis, so Area = (1/2)(1)(1) = 0.5.

steps.title: Compute the probability
steps.apply: P(X + Y <= 1) = (area of triangle) / (area of square) = 0.5 / 4 = 0.125.
steps.note: Equivalently, P = integral over R of f(x,y) dx dy = (1/4) * 0.5 = 0.125.

questions.formula_note: Geometric probability applies because the joint distribution is uniform over a rectangle; the required probability equals the ratio of the favorable triangle area to the total square area.

questions.question:
Let Y = Z^2, where Z = (X - mu) / sigma, and X is a normal random variable with mean mu and variance sigma^2. The variance of Y is

A. 1
B. 2
C. 3
D. 4

understand.plain: Z = (X - mu)/sigma is the standardized version of X, so Z ~ N(0,1). Y = Z^2 is then a chi-squared random variable with 1 degree of freedom. The variance of a chi-squared(1) variable is 2, which can be verified by computing E[Z^4] - (E[Z^2])^2 = 3 - 1 = 2 using the moments of the standard normal.

keywords.explain: Standardizing X by subtracting its mean and dividing by its standard deviation gives Z with mean 0 and variance 1.
The square of a standard normal variable follows a chi-squared distribution with 1 degree of freedom: Z^2 ~ chi^2(1), which has mean 1 and variance 2.
keywords.example: Z^2 takes only non-negative values; E[Z^2] = 1 and Var(Z^2) = 2.

keywords.explain: For Z ~ N(0,1): E[Z] = 0, E[Z^2] = 1, E[Z^3] = 0, E[Z^4] = 3. These are the standard moment formulas.
keywords.example: E[Z^4] = 3 is used to compute Var(Z^2) = E[Z^4] - (E[Z^2])^2 = 3 - 1 = 2.

understand.visual_alt: Z is the standardized normal, so Y = Z^2 is chi-squared(1) with variance 2.

given.aim: Find Var(Y) where Y = Z^2 and Z = (X - mu)/sigma with X ~ N(mu, sigma^2).

terms.connects: Reduces the problem to finding Var(Z^2) for a standard normal

terms.meaning: Square of a standard normal; Y ~ chi^2(1)
Variance formula: second moment minus square of first moment
terms.connects: Requires the 4th moment of Z, which is 3 for N(0,1)

given.plan: Note Z ~ N(0,1), so Y = Z^2. Compute E[Y] = E[Z^2] = 1 and E[Y^2] = E[Z^4] = 3, then Var(Y) = 3 - 1^2 = 2.

steps.title: Identify Z as a standard normal
steps.formula_raw: Z = (X - mu) / sigma, X ~ N(mu, sigma^2) => Z ~ N(0, 1)
steps.apply: Since X has mean mu and variance sigma^2, Z = (X - mu)/sigma has mean (mu - mu)/sigma = 0 and variance sigma^2/sigma^2 = 1. Thus Z ~ N(0,1).
steps.note: This standardization step removes all parameters; the result for Var(Y) will be a pure number.

steps.title: Compute E[Y] = E[Z^2]
steps.note: Thus E[Y] = 1, consistent with Y ~ chi^2(1) having mean equal to its degree of freedom.

steps.title: Compute E[Y^2] = E[Z^4] using the 4th moment of N(0,1)
steps.formula_raw: E[Z^4] = 3 (4th raw moment of standard normal)
steps.apply: For Z ~ N(0,1), the k-th raw moment is E[Z^k] = (k-1)!! for even k. For k = 4: E[Z^4] = 3!! = 3. So E[Y^2] = E[Z^4] = 3.

steps.title: Apply the variance formula
steps.note: The answer is 2, which matches option B. This is also the known result Var(chi^2(1)) = 2*1 = 2.

questions.formula_note: The solution uses the variance decomposition formula Var(Y) = E[Y^2] - (E[Y])^2 and the standard moments of the normal distribution E[Z^2] = 1 and E[Z^4] = 3.
